question_title: How many total goals will be scored across all 72 group-stage matches of the 2026 FIFA World Cup?
summary: The 2026 FIFA World Cup group stage has 20 of 72 matches completed with 62 goals (3.1 goals/match), well above the 2010-2022 historical average of ~2.5 gpm. The elevated rate is partly structural — the 48-team format introduces weaker teams (Curacao, Cape Verde, Haiti) creating more blowouts — and partly typical MD1 inflation. Monte Carlo simulations across three scenarios (strong regression, moderate format effect, rate persistence) weighted 20/50/30 yield a median of ~207 total goals. The 62 already-banked goals constrain the lower tail. The key uncertainty is whether the remaining 52 matches regress toward ~2.5 gpm or remain elevated at ~2.8-3.0 gpm due to format effects.

factors.description: 20 completed MD1 matches show 3.1 goals/match, significantly above historical 2.5 gpm. 48-team format creates structural mismatches (Germany 7-1 Curacao, Sweden 5-1 Tunisia)
Historical regression: World Cup group stages 2010-2022 averaged 2.50 gpm. MD1 tends to be slightly elevated vs later matchdays. Some early blowouts are outliers.
48-team format is genuinely new - weaker teams play all 3 matchdays, maintaining mismatch effect throughout. This is structural, not noise.
MD3 dead rubber effect is reduced because 32/48 teams advance (top 2 + 8 best 3rd), so most MD3 matches still matter competitively
62 goals already banked from 20 matches constrains the lower tail and anchors the total upward from pure historical projection

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary: 2026 FIFA World Cup Group Stage Goals

**Median estimate: 207 total goals** (80% CI: 184–231)

### Key findings:
- **20 of 72 matches completed** with 62 goals scored (3.1 goals/match) — well above the historical average of ~2.5 gpm
- The **48-team format creates structural mismatches** (Germany 7-1 Curaçao, Sweden 5-1 Tunisia) that will persist across all matchdays
- However, **some regression is expected** — MD1 historically runs slightly hot, and teams adapt tactically
- The **generous advancement rules** (32/48 teams advance) mean most MD3 matches still matter competitively, reducing the "dead rubber" low-scoring effect

### Distribution:
| Percentile | Goals |
|-----------|-------|
| P10 | 184 |
| P25 | 195 |
| P50 | **207** |
| P75 | 220 |
| P90 | 231 |

The median of 207 implies a tournament-wide group stage average of ~2.88 goals/match — above the historical 2.5 but below the current MD1 rate of 3.1, reflecting expected partial regression.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) How many total goals will be scored across all 72 group-stage matches of the 2026 FIFA World Cup?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44099)
resolution_criteria: This question asks for the total number of goals scored across all 72 group-stage matches of the 2026 FIFA World Cup (matches played June 11–27, 2026). It resolves to the cumulative goal count, counting goals by both teams in every group-stage match (own goals count toward the scoring team's total as recorded in the official scoreline; penalty-shootout goals do not apply, as group-stage matches that end level are recorded as draws). The resolution value is the goal total reported for the completed group stage on the "2026 FIFA World Cup" Wikipedia article (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup), which displays a running "Goals scored" figure; once all 72 group matches are complete this figure equals the group-stage total. For example, a total of 190 goals resolves as 190.
fine_print: Only goals scored in the 72 group-stage matches count; knockout-stage matches (which begin June 28, 2026) are excluded. If the Wikipedia "Goals scored" figure reflects matches beyond the group stage at the time of resolution, the resolver will sum the goals from the 72 published group-stage scorelines (each group's match results) instead. If a group-stage match is abandoned and replayed or its result is administratively altered, the final official scoreline as recorded by FIFA governs. If the Wikipedia article is unavailable or ambiguous, the goal total will be determined from the official FIFA tournament statistics or other [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions). Goals awarded by forfeit (e.g., a 3–0 walkover) count as recorded in the official result.

factors.description: 20 completed MD1 matches show 3.1 goals/match, significantly above historical 2.5 gpm. 48-team format creates structural mismatches (Germany 7-1 Curacao, Sweden 5-1 Tunisia)
Historical regression: World Cup group stages 2010-2022 averaged 2.50 gpm. MD1 tends to be slightly elevated vs later matchdays. Some early blowouts are outliers.
48-team format is genuinely new - weaker teams (Curacao, Cape Verde, etc.) play all 3 matchdays, maintaining mismatch effect throughout. This is structural, not noise.
MD3 dead rubber effect is reduced because 32/48 teams advance (top 2 + 8 best 3rd), so most MD3 matches still matter competitively
62 goals already banked from 20 matches - this is known and reduces overall uncertainty

input.anchor: Historical WC group stage: ~2.5 gpm × 72 matches = ~180 goals. Adjusted for 48-team format mismatches: ~2.7 × 72 = ~194. But 62 goals already scored in 20 matches shifts floor up.
input.assessment: The 2026 World Cup group stage has seen elevated scoring in its first 20 matches (3.1 gpm vs historical ~2.5). The key question is whether this persists or regresses. The 48-team format creates genuine structural differences - weaker teams like Curacao, Cape Verde, and Haiti are playing against top nations, producing more blowouts. However, some regression toward the mean is likely for MD2 and MD3. My Monte Carlo simulations using both scenario-mixture and refined single-model approaches converge on a median of ~205-208 total goals, with an 80% CI of roughly 184-231. The already-banked 62 goals constrain the lower tail significantly.
input.calibration_notes: The 62 goals in 20 matches is locked in. For remaining 52 matches, even at the low end (2.3 gpm), total would be 182. At historical rate of 2.5, total = 192. The median estimate of 207 implies remaining rate of ~2.79 gpm, which is plausible given format effects. Distribution width of ~47 goals (P10-P90) seems appropriate for 52 remaining matches with rate uncertainty.
input.key_uncertainties: Main uncertainty: whether the elevated MD1 scoring rate (3.1) reflects format-driven structural change or random variance. The 20-match sample is moderate - could easily be 0.3 gpm off the true rate in either direction. Secondary: matchday-specific effects (MD3 dynamics in the new format are completely unprecedented).
input.tool_audit: research() returned excellent detailed data on 20 completed matches with individual scorelines. execute_code was essential for running Monte Carlo simulations. Metaculus question data provided good context. No tool failures.
input.process_reflection: The research tool performed well, finding detailed match-by-match results. The Monte Carlo simulation framework works naturally for this kind of partial-data extrapolation problem. One friction point: the initial research report found only 10 matches, but the second query found 20 - showing the value of parallel research queries. The scenario mixture approach is well-suited for this kind of structural uncertainty (is the format effect real?).

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

**Hallucinated evidence**: All factual claims in the 5 factors are grounded in the research trace. The trace confirms 20 completed MD1 matches with 62 goals (3.1 gpm), specific blowout scores (Germany 7-1 Curaçao, Sweden 5-1 Tunisia), historical group-stage averages (2010-2022: 2.10, 2.83, 2.54, 2.50 gpm), and the 32/48 advancement format. No hallucinations found.

**Data discrepancy (minor, no impact)**: The research tool returned "61 goals" in its summary but the individual match scores sum to 62. The agent caught this discrepancy in code execution (line 233 of trace: "Sum: 62, Count: 20, Mean: 3.100") and correctly used 62 for all subsequent simulations. The first simulation briefly used 61 but this was corrected immediately.

**Double-counting**: Factors 1 and 5 both reference the 62 goals in 20 matches, but serve different analytical purposes — Factor 1 establishes the elevated rate, Factor 5 constrains the lower tail via known floor. Factors 1 and 3 are related (both argue for elevated scoring from format mismatches) but Factor 1 is empirical (observed data) while Factor 3 is structural (format argument for persistence). These are distinct enough not to constitute problematic double-counting.

**Wrong-direction factors**: Factor 2 (regression to mean, logit=-0.8) correctly pushes downward. All other factors correctly push upward. No wrong-direction issues.

**Resolution criteria**: Clearly stated and properly engaged. The agent forecasts total goals across all 72 group-stage matches, consistent with the Wikipedia resolution source.

**Assessment consistency**: The narrative assessment (median ~205-208) is consistent with the factors and the final estimate (center 207). The Monte Carlo simulations converge on this range across multiple modeling approaches (scenario mixture P50=205, refined model P50=208, averaged P50=206).

**Counterargument quality**: Strong. Cites specific 2014 analogy (MD1 at 3.1 settling to 2.83 overall), identifies concrete mechanisms (tactical adaptation by debutant teams), and provides specific triggers for updating (MD2 Day 1 scoring rate). Not a token gesture.

**Confidence calibration**: 0.45 is appropriate — substantial real data (20 matches) but 52 remaining with genuine uncertainty about rate regression.

**Probability assessment**: The center of 207 implies a remaining-match rate of ~2.79 gpm, which is a reasonable Bayesian posterior given the prior (~2.5 historical) updated by 20 observations at 3.1, with structural reasoning for the format effect. The 80% CI of ~184-231 captures appropriate uncertainty. I estimate a similar center (205-210) independently from the evidence, so no meaningful divergence.

**Anchor divergence**: Anchor at ~194 (adjusted historical), final at 207 — a 13-point departure driven primarily by Factor 1 (observed 3.1 gpm in 20 matches, logit=+1.0, confidence=0.8). This is a strong, well-evidenced factor that justifies the departure.

**Tool audit**: Agent claims "No tool failures." Ground truth confirms 0 errors across 9 calls. Consistent.

**Worldview consistency**: The worldview research entries confirm the same data (61-62 goals in 20 matches, 3.05-3.1 gpm). No contradictions found.
